name: Honduran Baleada
steps: Warm the tortillas and the mashed pinto beans.
Spread the beans down the center of each tortilla.
Top with the crumbled queso fresco and sour cream, then fold in half.
steps_detailed: Warm the pinto beans in a small pot, mashing lightly until spreadable but still a little chunky.
Warm each tortilla in a dry skillet with a light scrape of butter until soft and lightly toasted in spots, about 30 seconds per side.
Spread a generous layer of the mashed beans down the center of each tortilla.
Top with the crumbled queso fresco, a drizzle of sour cream, and the sliced avocado.
Fold each tortilla in half and press gently to close before serving.

name: Honduran Tapado Costeño
steps: Simmer the cassava in the coconut milk with a cup of water for 10 minutes.
Add the plantain and cook another 10 minutes until both are tender.
Add the shrimp and simmer 4-5 minutes until just cooked through.
steps_detailed: Bring the coconut milk, 1.5 cups water, onion, bell pepper, garlic, and achiote paste to a simmer in a wide pot.
Add the cassava and simmer 12-15 minutes until it starts to soften.
Add the plantain and continue simmering 8-10 minutes until both the cassava and plantain are fork-tender.
Add the shrimp and simmer gently 4-5 minutes, just until pink and cooked through - don't let it boil hard or the shrimp will toughen.
Stir in the cilantro just before serving.

name: Honduran Yuca con Chicharron
steps: Boil the cassava chunks 20 minutes until fork-tender; drain.
Fry the pork cubes in their own fat until deeply browned and crisp.
Serve the yuca topped with the crispy pork and shredded cabbage, with the lime juice squeezed over.
steps_detailed: Boil the cassava in salted water 20-25 minutes until completely fork-tender; drain well.
Season the pork cubes with black pepper. Heat the vegetable oil in a heavy skillet over medium heat and add the pork.
Cook the pork slowly, 15-18 minutes, letting its own fat render out, until the pieces are deeply browned and crisp on the outside - stir occasionally.
Toss the shredded cabbage, carrot, and red onion with the lime juice for a quick slaw.
Serve the boiled cassava topped with the crispy pork and slaw piled on top.

name: Honduran Casamiento
steps: Saute the onion in the oil until softened.
Add the rice and beans, and stir-fry 4-5 minutes until well combined.
Serve with eggs.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion and garlic in the oil over medium heat, 4-5 minutes, until softened.
Add the rice and beans, and stir-fry 5-6 minutes until well combined and lightly toasted.
Top with cilantro and serve with eggs - casamiento, meaning 'marriage,' refers to the union of rice and beans, a Honduran breakfast staple.

name: Honduran Rosquillas with Cheese
steps: Mix the masa harina with the crumbled queso fresco and butter into a firm dough.
Shape into small rings and bake at 375F for 18-22 minutes until golden.
steps_detailed: Mix the masa harina with the crumbled queso fresco, butter, and egg into a firm, cohesive dough.
Shape into small rings, about 2 inches across.
Bake at 375F (190C) for 20-25 minutes until golden and slightly crisp - rosquillas, cheesy corn rings, are a beloved Honduran breakfast and coffee-time snack.

name: Honduran Fried Plantains with Cream
steps: Fry the plantain slices in the oil until golden.
Serve with a dollop of cream.
steps_detailed: Fry the plantain slices in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until deeply golden and caramelized.
Serve warm with a generous dollop of cream - fried plantains with crema are a common, comforting Honduran breakfast.

name: Honduran Catrachas

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour
Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps: Fry the tortilla until crisp.
Spread with the refried beans and top with the queso fresco.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Cassava Flour Grain Free Tortillas, Cassava Flour
Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

ingredients_full.note: as a crema stand-in, to top

steps_detailed: Fry the tortilla in a little oil until crisp and flat.
Spread generously with the refried beans, then top with the crumbled queso fresco and a drizzle of cream.
Catrachas, named affectionately for Hondurans themselves, are a simple, beloved everyday snack-lunch.

name: Honduran Sopa de Caracol
steps: Simmer the potato and carrot in the coconut milk with a splash of water for 12-15 minutes.
Add the shrimp and simmer 5-6 minutes until just cooked through.
Serve hot with rice.
steps_detailed: Simmer the potato, carrot, and onion in the coconut milk with 1/2 cup water for 14-18 minutes until nearly tender.
Add the shrimp (standing in for conch) and simmer gently 5-7 minutes until just cooked through.
Top with cilantro and serve hot with rice - sopa de caracol, a rich coconut conch soup, is a treasured specialty of Honduras's Caribbean coast and Bay Islands.

name: Honduran Anafres

ingredients.ingredient: Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps: Warm the refried beans in the oil.
Top generously with the queso fresco and warm until melted.
Serve with tortilla chips.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps_detailed: Warm the refried beans in the oil in a small oven-safe dish.
Top generously with the crumbled queso fresco and jalapeno if using.
Broil 2-3 minutes until the cheese is melted and bubbling.
Serve hot with tortilla chips for dipping - anafres, traditionally served bubbling in a small clay pot over coals, is a beloved shared appetizer.

name: Honduran Pollo con Tajadas
steps: Fry the chicken until golden and cooked through.
Fry the plantain slices until crisp.
Serve together with the shredded cabbage.
steps_detailed: Fry the chicken in the oil over medium heat, 6-8 minutes per side, until golden and cooked through.
Fry the green plantain slices until crisp and golden, about 3-4 minutes per side.

Serve together with the shredded cabbage and diced tomatoes, finished with a squeeze of lime - pollo con tajadas is a beloved Honduran fast-food classic, sold everywhere from street stalls to family restaurants.

name: Honduran Carne Asada Hondureña

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw
Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps: Marinate the beef with the garlic and lime juice for at least 20 minutes.
Grill or pan-fry until well charred and cooked through.
Serve with the refried beans and tortillas.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw
Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps_detailed: Marinate the thinly sliced beef with the garlic and lime juice for at least 25-30 minutes.
Grill or pan-fry in the oil over high heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until well charred outside.
Saute the onion until softened and serve alongside.
Serve with the refried beans, warm tortillas, and grilled onion - a classic Honduran grilled beef dinner.

name: Honduran Nacatamales
steps: Whisk the masa harina with warm water into a thick dough.
Brown the pork with the tomatoes and potato.
Spread the dough on parchment or a soaked corn husk, top with the filling, fold into a packet, and steam 1 hour.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Whisk the masa harina with warm water and the oil into a thick, smooth dough.
Brown the pork with the diced tomatoes and potato, 10-12 minutes, then fold in the raisins.
Spread a layer of the masa dough onto parchment or a soaked corn husk, top with the pork filling, fold into a sealed packet.
Steam 1-1.25 hours until firm and fully cooked - nacatamales are a beloved Honduran holiday tradition, especially at Christmas.

name: Honduran Pastelitos
steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Make a simple dough from the flour and water, roll thin, fill, and fold into half-moons.
Fry until golden and crisp.
steps_detailed: Brown the beef with the onion and diced tomatoes.
Make a simple, firm dough from the flour, a pinch of salt, and water, and roll out thin.
Cut into rounds, fill with the beef mixture, and fold into half-moons, crimping well.
Fry until deeply golden and crisp, 4-5 minutes - pastelitos are a beloved Honduran fried snack.

name: Honduran Baleadas Sencillas

ingredients.ingredient: Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps: Warm the tortilla.
Spread with the refried beans and top with the crumbled queso fresco, folding closed.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Frijoles rojos volteados (Refried beans, red, canned)

steps_detailed: Warm the tortilla until soft and pliable.
Spread with the refried beans, then the crumbled queso fresco and a drizzle of cream, folding in half.
A simpler version of the classic baleada, eaten any time of day as a quick snack across Honduras.

name: Honduran Yuca Frita
steps: Boil the cassava until just tender, about 12-15 minutes, then cut into fries.
Fry in the oil until golden and crisp.
Finish with the lime juice.
steps_detailed: Boil the cassava until just tender, 14-18 minutes, then drain, cool slightly, and cut into fries.
Fry in the oil over medium-high heat with the garlic, 5-7 minutes, until deeply golden and crisp.
Finish with a generous squeeze of lime - fried yuca is a beloved Honduran side and snack.

name: Honduran Torrejas Hondureñas

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Graham Crackers Flavored With Honey, Honey

steps: Dip the crackers in the beaten egg.
Fry until golden on both sides.
Warm the brown sugar with a splash of water and cinnamon into a syrup, and pour over.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Graham Crackers Flavored With Honey, Honey

steps_detailed: Dip the crackers in the beaten egg, coating well.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, 2-3 minutes per side, until golden.
Warm the brown sugar with a splash of water and the cinnamon into a light syrup, and pour generously over the fried torrejas - a Honduran holiday sweet, especially for Christmas.

name: Honduran Horchata Pudding
steps: Simmer the rice in the milk with the sugar and cinnamon for 20-25 minutes until thick.
Chill before serving.
steps_detailed: Simmer the rice in the milk with the sugar, cinnamon, and vanilla over medium-low heat, stirring often, 22-28 minutes until thick and creamy.
Chill before serving - a pudding-style nod to horchata, the beloved rice-and-cinnamon drink found throughout Honduras.

name: Honduran Montucas

ingredients.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps: Blend the corn with the masa harina and sugar into a thick batter.
Fold in the crumbled queso fresco.
Wrap portions in foil and steam 35-40 minutes until firm.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps_detailed: Blend the corn with the masa harina, sugar, and melted butter into a thick, cohesive batter.
Fold in the crumbled queso fresco.
Wrap portions tightly in foil (traditionally corn husk) and steam 38-45 minutes until firm and set.
Montucas, sweet corn tamales, are a beloved Honduran snack, especially popular during the corn harvest season.

name: Honduran Chimol
steps: Combine the tomatoes, onion, cilantro, and lime juice.
Season with salt and serve alongside grilled meats.
steps_detailed: Combine the tomatoes, onion, cilantro, lime juice, and jalapeno.
Season with salt and let sit 10 minutes - chimol, a simple fresh salsa, tops nearly everything grilled in Honduras.

name: Honduran Crema Hondureña
steps: Whisk the cream cheese with the milk and lime juice until smooth and pourable.
Drizzle over tacos, beans, or fried plantains.
steps_detailed: Whisk the cream cheese with the milk, lime juice, and oil until smooth and pourable, adding more milk if a thinner consistency is wanted.
Crema, tangy and rich, is drizzled over nearly everything in Honduran cooking, from beans to fried plantains.
